title: Relevant Costs and Decision Making
description: Learn how managers identify relevant costs when making business decisions. Understand the difference between relevant and irrelevant costs, opportunity costs, sunk costs and how these concepts support effective managerial decision making.

sections.title: Learning Objectives
items: Understand what makes a cost relevant.
Differentiate between relevant and irrelevant costs.
Explain the concept of sunk costs and why they should be ignored.
Understand opportunity costs and their role in decision making.
Apply relevant cost analysis to common managerial decisions.
Recognize the limitations of quantitative analysis and the importance of qualitative factors.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Managers make decisions every day, such as whether to accept a special order, manufacture a product internally, discontinue a product line or invest in new equipment.
Not every cost should influence these decisions. Only costs and revenues that change between alternatives are relevant.
Relevant cost analysis helps managers focus only on information that affects the outcome.

sections.title: What Is a Relevant Cost?
content: A relevant cost is a future cost that differs between decision alternatives.
If a cost will be the same regardless of the decision, it is irrelevant.
Relevant costs help managers compare alternatives objectively.

sections.title: Example: Choosing Between Two Suppliers
content: Supplier A charges $45 per unit.
Supplier B charges $50 per unit but includes free delivery.
Delivery costs differ between the alternatives, making both purchase price and transportation costs relevant.

sections.title: Irrelevant Costs
content: Irrelevant costs do not change regardless of the decision.
These costs should not influence managerial decisions.
Many accounting reports contain irrelevant information for specific decisions.

sections.title: Example: Factory Rent
content: A company pays $20,000 per month in factory rent.
Whether management accepts one additional customer order or not, the rent remains unchanged.
Therefore, the rent is irrelevant for this particular decision.

sections.title: Sunk Costs
content: A sunk cost is a cost that has already been incurred and cannot be recovered.
Because sunk costs cannot be changed by future decisions, they are always irrelevant.
Managers should ignore sunk costs when evaluating alternatives.

sections.title: Example: Software Development
content: A company has already spent $500,000 developing software.
Management must now decide whether to launch the product.
The development cost is a sunk cost and should not influence the launch decision.

sections.title: Opportunity Costs
content: An opportunity cost is the benefit lost by choosing one alternative instead of another.
Opportunity costs may not appear in accounting records, but they are often highly relevant.
Managers should consider what they give up when selecting an alternative.

sections.title: Example: Limited Production Capacity
content: A factory can only produce one of two products.
Choosing Product A means giving up the profit that Product B could have generated.
That lost profit is the opportunity cost.

sections.title: Incremental Costs and Revenues
content: Incremental costs are additional costs that result from choosing one alternative.
Incremental revenues are additional revenues generated by the same decision.
Managers compare incremental revenues with incremental costs to determine whether a decision adds value.

sections.title: Example: Accepting a Special Order
content: A customer offers to purchase 5,000 units at a discounted price.
If the company has unused production capacity, only the additional variable costs and revenues are relevant.
Existing fixed costs remain unchanged and are therefore irrelevant.

sections.title: Make-or-Buy Decisions
content: Companies often decide whether to manufacture a component internally or purchase it from an external supplier.
Relevant costs include avoidable manufacturing costs, supplier prices and opportunity costs.
Unavoidable fixed costs should generally be ignored.

sections.title: Example: Purchasing Components
content: A supplier offers to sell a component for $18 each.
Internal production costs $20 per unit, but $4 consists of unavoidable fixed costs.
The relevant manufacturing cost is only $16, making internal production the cheaper alternative.

sections.title: Keep or Discontinue a Product
content: Managers sometimes consider eliminating products that appear unprofitable.
Only costs that can actually be avoided are relevant.
Allocated fixed costs often continue even if the product is discontinued.

sections.title: Example: Product Line Decision
content: A product line shows a small accounting loss.
However, most of the allocated fixed costs will remain after discontinuation.
Eliminating the product may actually reduce total company profit.

sections.title: Qualitative Factors
content: Financial calculations alone are not enough.
Managers should also consider quality, customer satisfaction, employee morale, supplier reliability, environmental impact and long-term strategy.
These qualitative factors may outweigh small financial differences.

sections.title: Example: Choosing a Supplier
content: Supplier A is slightly cheaper.
Supplier B has an excellent reliability record and delivers faster.
Management may choose Supplier B if reliable deliveries reduce operational risk.

sections.title: Shipping Example
content: A vessel operator receives a discounted charter offer during a slow freight market.
Relevant costs include additional fuel, port charges, canal tolls, crew overtime and other voyage-specific costs.
Existing vessel depreciation and management salaries are largely irrelevant if they remain unchanged.
Management should also consider opportunity costs if accepting the charter prevents taking a more profitable contract later.

sections.title: Example: Dry Bulk Charter
content: A Capesize vessel can accept a charter today for $18,000 per day or wait for the possibility of securing a $23,000 per day contract next week.
Management compares expected contribution margins, market uncertainty and the opportunity cost of waiting before making the decision.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Including sunk costs in future decisions.
Ignoring opportunity costs.
Treating all fixed costs as relevant.
Using accounting allocations instead of avoidable costs.
Ignoring important qualitative factors.

sections.title: Limitations
content: Relevant cost analysis assumes managers can accurately estimate future costs and revenues.
Market conditions, customer behavior and uncertainty may affect actual outcomes.
Many important strategic considerations cannot be measured precisely in financial terms.

sections.title: Expert Insight
content: One of the most common mistakes in business is allowing past investments to influence future decisions. Experienced managers recognize that only future costs and benefits that differ between alternatives should affect decisions. This disciplined approach leads to more rational and profitable decision making.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
items: Relevant costs are future costs that differ between alternatives.
Irrelevant costs remain unchanged regardless of the decision.
Sunk costs should always be ignored.
Opportunity costs represent benefits given up by choosing one alternative over another.
Incremental analysis compares additional revenues with additional costs.
Qualitative factors should always complement financial analysis.
Good managerial decisions combine relevant cost analysis with strategic thinking.

sections.title: Reflection
questions: Why should sunk costs never influence future decisions?
Can the financially cheapest alternative still be the wrong decision? Why?
What opportunity costs might a shipping company face when accepting a long-term charter?
